lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market outpatient centers with medical staff repeatedly delivering general or specialized care to external patients and payors, including multispecialty community clinics, pain therapy centers, sleep disorder centers, and biofeedback centers.
lens.excluded_activities: Physician, dentist, or other practitioner offices classified elsewhere; family planning, outpatient mental health or substance use, HMO, dialysis, surgical, emergency, laboratory, imaging, and home-health providers classified elsewhere; captive units, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and nonprofit or public centers without transferable control economics.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Centers provide recurring visits, diagnostics, treatment, care coordination, and monitoring to patients. Revenue can combine Medicare or Medicaid prospective or fee-schedule payments, commercial insurer contracts, patient cost sharing, grants, and sliding-fee payments; the mix varies sharply between community health centers and commercial specialty clinics.

a.bridge: BLS measures all NAICS 621400 outpatient centers rather than 621498, whose community, pain, sleep, biofeedback, and multispecialty clinic mix is not separately reported. I map intake, scheduling, eligibility, billing, coding, chart and referral summaries, prior authorization, patient messaging, translation, discharge and care-plan drafting, quality reporting, and population outreach to exposed work, then discount for diagnosis, examination, therapy, specimen collection, equipment operation, patient education, and hands-on care. The broader table includes dialysis, surgery, emergency, and behavioral-health centers and may overstate clinical intensity relative to some 621498 clinics while understating it for complex community centers.
a.rationale: BLS reports that broader outpatient care centers have 15.71% office and administrative support employment, including billing, reception, customer service, medical secretaries, and records work, alongside 41.13% healthcare practitioners and technical occupations and 11.19% healthcare support. AMA reports active use of AI for research summaries, notes, billing codes, discharge or care-plan drafts, chart summaries, portal responses, translation, and assistive diagnosis. This creates material exposure in documentation and coordination, but most clinical wages remain only partly substitutable.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted task study stratified among community health, pain, sleep, biofeedback, and multispecialty outpatient centers, including employed and contracted clinicians and measuring current automation in intake, revenue cycle, documentation, referrals, care management, outreach, and direct care.
caveats: No six-digit occupation or payroll-by-function table was located.
The code's clinic types have materially different practitioner, technician, and administrative mixes.
Physician adoption measures use of tools, not wage displacement or avoided hiring.

rho.bridge: AMA surveys U.S. physicians across practice settings, not 621498 centers or the share of exposed wages implemented over five years. I convert the reported use of documentation, summarization, messaging, translation, diagnosis support, and authorization tools into implementation potential, then discount for EHR integration, privacy, clinical validation, liability, payer rules, fragmented grants and reporting systems, workforce training, safety-net budgets, and human review. The physician sample may overstate awareness at small clinics but understate adoption by networks that can standardize centralized workflows.
rho.rationale: AMA's January-February 2026 survey reports that 72% of physicians explicitly use at least one AI application and that documentation and summarization lead adoption. Physicians also prioritize validated safety and efficacy, privacy assurance, liability clarity, training, and involvement in adoption decisions. Five years permits wider implementation of clerical and assistive workflows, but self-reported current use does not imply reliable end-to-end production or autonomous care.
rho.change_evidence: A production cohort of independent and safety-net 621498 centers documenting baseline labor, deployed workflows, EHR and payer integration, clinician review, denial and rework rates, privacy or safety incidents, training cost, patient access, and avoided hiring for at least twelve months.
caveats: AMA does not isolate center type, ownership, size, or NAICS code.
Reported use includes research and drafting that may save minutes without eliminating positions.
Underfunded community centers may have high need but limited implementation capacity.

e.rationale: Census confirms that the code consists of medical-staffed outpatient centers serving patients, which fits an external recurring service. Eligibility is reduced because the supplied population can include nonprofit community health centers, public or tribal programs, grant-dependent entities, hospital or health-system affiliates, clinics whose economics are inseparable from practitioner groups, and operations whose broad receipts do not create transferable LMM EBITDA. HRSA alone reports nearly 1,400 funded health-center organizations operating more than 16,000 sites, demonstrating a large safety-net segment, although not all HRSA centers are classified in 621498 and SUSB firms may not capture that population consistently. Commercial pain, sleep, biofeedback, and multispecialty centers support a meaningful eligible share.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level match of SUSB-band entities to IRS tax status, HRSA awardee and look-alike data, CMS enrollment and ownership, state facility licenses, hospital affiliations, clinic websites, payer mix, grants, and normalized EBITDA, coded by 621498 clinic type and transferable control.
caveats: No source measures the share of LMM-band 621498 firms that are transferable commercial operators.
HRSA health centers overlap 621498 imperfectly and their sites are not equivalent to firms.
The supplied firm count is margin-bridged using a hospital and healthcare-facilities margin that may misclassify safety-net and specialty centers.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures five-year ownership intentions for U.S. employer businesses across industries, and GAO documents physician-practice consolidation rather than completed control transfers of 621498 centers. I start below Gallup's 22% intention rate because nonprofit, grant-supported, public, affiliated, and clinician-dependent centers are less likely to undergo a qualifying sale, while allowing an upper channel for specialty-clinic roll-ups and MSO-backed acquisitions. This may understate transfer activity in attractive pain or multispecialty niches and overstate completed deals because intentions fail and affiliations can occur without control transfer.
s5.rationale: Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. GAO documents hospital, insurer, corporate, and private-equity acquisition and affiliation channels, including roll-ups through management services organizations, but also notes that many smaller transactions are not reported and that effects and prevalence vary by specialty. Neither source provides a 621498 transaction denominator.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year transaction census from CMS and state change-of-ownership records, PitchBook, LevinPro HC, Irving Levin Associates, HRSA scope changes, and purchase agreements, matched to 621498 clinic type and EBITDA and separating control sales from affiliations, MSO service contracts, nonprofit combinations, internal reorganizations, and closures.
caveats: Broad owner intentions are not completed healthcare control transfers.
Physician-practice consolidation is adjacent to, but not the same as, acquisition of a staffed outpatient center.
Corporate-practice, nonprofit, licensure, grant, and payer rules can prevent or reshape control transactions.

q.bridge: CMS describes the encounter-based Medicare FQHC prospective payment system, and GAO studies price effects of consolidated physician practices; neither measures five-year retention of AI benefit by LMM 621498 centers. I treat fixed encounter or contracted payments as allowing some near-term retention of lower internal cost, then discount for annual market-basket updates, Medicaid and grant constraints, commercial renewal, value-based sharing, sliding fees, patient competition, mission-driven access expansion, clinician compensation, and added care volume. FQHC evidence likely understates pricing discretion at commercial specialty centers and overstates the safety-net share of the SUSB firm population.
q.rationale: CMS pays FQHC visits at the lesser of charges or a geographically adjusted national prospective rate and updates the base annually; the 2026 base is $207.72. That structure can preserve a cost reduction within a paid encounter but limits price setting. GAO reports that consolidation sometimes raises commercial prices, with large variation across specialties. Many community centers are likely to convert productivity into access, care coordination, or compliance rather than distributable margin, while commercial pain and sleep clinics may retain more.
q.change_evidence: Center-level payer contracts and encounter economics showing Medicare, Medicaid, commercial, grant, and self-pay revenue; staffing and administrative cost; quality incentives; denial expense; access commitments; automation changes; repricing; patient volume; and retained margin by clinic type over several contract cycles.
caveats: FQHC payment is only one revenue model inside a heterogeneous six-digit code.
No source measures AI-benefit retention, competitive pass-through, or reinvestment for these centers.
Grant restrictions and safety-net missions can turn labor productivity into service expansion rather than owner cash flow.

d5.bridge: BLS projects constant-price output for all NAICS 621400 outpatient care centers, and HRSA measures patients and visits at federally funded health centers rather than the narrower 621498 commercial and safety-net mix. I translate the broader 3.5% annual real-output projection and recent health-center patient growth into a five-year quantity range, tempering for funding volatility, workforce shortages, payer access controls, site shifts, telehealth, heterogeneous specialty demand, and the risk that real output reflects higher-intensity care rather than more visits. The broad forecast may overstate slower niches and understate community or specialty demand in underserved markets.
d5.rationale: BLS projects outpatient-care-center real output from $217.8 billion in 2024 to $307.4 billion in 2034 and employment growth of 18.8%. HRSA reports 32.4 million health-center patients and 139.4 million visits in 2024, while its program update shows patients rising from 28.6 million in 2020 to 32.4 million in 2024. Aging, chronic disease, access gaps, and continued outpatient delivery support growth, but five-year uncertainty warrants a low case only modestly above current quantity.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit constant-price revenue and visit indexes by community health, pain, sleep, biofeedback, and multispecialty center, linked to unique patients, intensity, payer mix, capacity, wait times, grants, closures, telehealth substitution, and a constant-service-basket forecast through 2031.
caveats: BLS combines 621498 with other outpatient center industries that have different growth drivers.
HRSA centers are not a complete or exact 621498 population and include nonprofit safety-net providers.
Real-output growth does not directly distinguish visit quantity, case complexity, and quality change.

o.bridge: Census establishes that these are medical-staffed outpatient centers and HRSA documents coordinated medical, dental, behavioral, preventive, and chronic-care delivery, but neither measures the share of year-five care still requiring a 621498 operator after telehealth, self-service, software, or migration to other provider settings. I preserve operator need for diagnosis, examinations, procedures, testing, multidisciplinary coordination, prescribing, quality accountability, vulnerable-patient support, and physical equipment, while allowing routine education, monitoring, intake, navigation, follow-up, and some low-acuity visits to shift to digital or other settings. The community-health evidence may overstate accountable-center need relative to simple commercial niches.
o.rationale: Census requires medical staff and general or specialized outpatient care, including multidisciplinary centers. HRSA reports 139.4 million visits and extensive chronic-disease management and screening, activities that require licensed providers and organizational accountability even when digitally assisted. AMA reports comfort with AI for routine information but much greater reluctance around diagnostic interpretation. Software can reduce or redirect some visits, but most current clinical quantity still needs an accountable provider organization.
o.change_evidence: A longitudinal claims, encounter, and patient-pathway panel classifying current 621498 services in 2031 as center-operated, telehealth-delivered by the same center, shifted to physician offices or hospitals, self-managed with software, or eliminated, stratified by clinic type, acuity, and payer.
caveats: The operator requirement differs substantially among pain, sleep, biofeedback, community, and multispecialty care.
Telehealth can preserve an accountable operator while reducing the need for a physical center location.
Care may migrate to physician practices, hospitals, retail clinics, or home settings even when total health demand grows.

narrative.executive_view: All other outpatient care centers offer a substantial documentation, billing, intake, referral, messaging, and care-coordination opportunity, but direct clinical work remains human and accountable. Demand is supportive, especially in community and chronic-care settings, yet the code mixes safety-net and commercial specialty models. Public and prospective payment, grant missions, nonprofit ownership, and heterogeneous clinic economics limit both retention and the clean transferable-firm pool.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft notes, coding suggestions, care plans, chart and referral summaries, portal replies, translations, authorization packages, outreach, scheduling, quality reports, and population-health worklists. It can assist clinical research and diagnosis under review. It is less able to replace examination, testing, therapy, prescribing accountability, multidisciplinary judgment, vulnerable-patient support, or hands-on treatment.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed encounter and contracted rates allow some productivity to remain within an episode, but public payment updates, Medicaid rules, grants, value-based sharing, commercial renewals, sliding fees, and access missions can recapture or redirect it. Commercial specialty clinics may retain more than community health centers, which may use freed capacity to serve additional patients rather than reduce payroll.
narrative.firm_availability: Commercial pain, sleep, biofeedback, and multispecialty clinics can be transferable service firms, and broader healthcare consolidation confirms buyer channels. The code also contains a large safety-net, nonprofit, public, affiliated, and grant-supported presence. Firm counts, sites, awardees, and control targets are therefore not interchangeable, and normalized EBITDA may be especially unreliable across this mix.
narrative.demand_durability: Broad outpatient output and health-center patient use are growing, supported by aging, chronic disease, access gaps, and continued migration to ambulatory care. AI and telehealth can alter the site and labor content of visits, but most diagnosis, treatment, monitoring, and coordinated care still requires a licensed organization. Funding and workforce constraints may limit realized volume despite underlying need.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key uncertainties are the six-digit clinic mix, nonprofit and affiliate share, contracted clinical labor, payer and grant composition, actual transfer history, and realized retained savings. Privacy, bias, unsafe recommendations, documentation errors, denials, cybersecurity, EHR fragmentation, clinician shortages, grant changes, Medicaid policy, and migration to other care settings can offset automation benefits.
narrative.principal_driver: Documentation and coordination-heavy recurring outpatient workflows can be automated while growing clinical demand continues to require accountable delivery organizations.
narrative.principal_weakness: The residual code combines commercial clinics with safety-net and nonprofit centers, making eligibility, value retention, and transferable control economics unusually uncertain.

sources.title: U.S. Census Bureau: 2022 NAICS Manual, 621498
sources.supports: Defines 621498 as medical-staffed centers providing general or specialized outpatient care and lists community health, pain therapy, sleep disorder, and biofeedback centers while identifying exclusions.

sources.title: BLS May 2023 OEWS: Outpatient Care Centers
sources.supports: Reports the broader outpatient-center occupation mix, including practitioners, nurses, healthcare support, medical assistants, and office, billing, reception, secretarial, and records roles.

sources.title: AMA 2026 Physician Survey on Augmented Intelligence
sources.supports: Reports current physician AI adoption and use cases plus privacy, validation, liability, training, workflow, clinical-accountability, and patient-use constraints.

sources.title: Gallup: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Reports that 22% of U.S. employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years, based on a fall 2024 survey.

sources.title: GAO-25-107450: Health Care Consolidation
sources.supports: Describes hospital, insurer, corporate, and private-equity acquisition or affiliation channels, MSO roll-ups, specialty variation, and commercial price effects in physician markets.

sources.title: BLS Employment and Output by Industry, 2024 to 2034
sources.supports: Projects broader NAICS 621400 employment from 1.115 million in 2024 to 1.325 million in 2034 and real output from $217.8 billion to $307.4 billion.

sources.title: HRSA Bureau of Primary Health Care: Health Center Program Impact
sources.supports: Reports 32.4 million health-center patients and 139.4 million visits in 2024, the safety-net population served, coordinated services, chronic-disease management, screening, and access role.

sources.title: CMS Medicare Learning Network: Federally Qualified Health Center
sources.supports: Explains encounter-based FQHC payment at the lesser of charges or a geographically adjusted prospective rate and reports the 2026 base rate and annual market-basket update.
